perturbation.original_text: Late payments of undisputed amounts shall accrue interest at a rate equal to two percent (2%) per annum, until paid. Such payments will be paid by Shipper to MPL within fifteen (15) Days of the invoice date or resolution of any dispute, if applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: Late payments of undisputed amounts may accrue interest. Such payments will be paid by Shipper to MPL at a time determined by MPL.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the defined interest rate and payment timeframe introduces uncertainty and contradicts Ohio law regarding payment terms. By removing the '2% per annum' interest rate and replacing the defined 15-day payment window with a timeline determined by the service provider, this modified contract puts the customer at legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: ORC § 1343.03 specifies the interest rate that applies in the absence of a written agreement. By removing the specific interest rate, the contract becomes vague. Also, removing the 15 day payment requirement allows MPL to make the payment time whenever they want, in breach of Ohio laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1343.03 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1343.03 | Rate not stipulated. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1343 Interest Effective: September 8, 2016 Latest Legislation: House Bill 166 - 131st General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) In cases other than those provided for in sections 1343.01 and 1343.02 of the Revised Code, when money becomes due and payable upon any bond, bill, note, or other instrument of writing, upon any book account, upon any settlement between parties, upon all verbal contracts entered into, and upon all judgments, decrees, and orders of any judicial tribunal for the payment of money arising out of tortious conduct or a contract or other transaction, the creditor is entitled to interest at the rate per annum determined pursuant to section 5703.47 of the Revised Code, unless a written contract provides a different rate of interest in relation to the money that becomes due and payable, in which case the creditor is entitled to interest at the rate provided in that contract. (B) Except as provided in divisions (C) and (D) of this section and subject to section 2325.18 of the Revised Code, interest on a judgment, decree, or order for the payment of money rendered in a civil action based on tortious conduct or a contract or other transaction, including, but not limited to a civil action based on tortious conduct or a contract or other transaction that has been settled by agreement of the parties, shall be computed from the date the judgment, decree, or order is rendered to the date on which the money is paid and shall be at the rate determined pursuant to section 5703.47 of the Revised Code that is in effect on the date the judgment,
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the specific interest rate and payment timeframe, leading to a contradiction of Ohio law. The original contract stated a '2% per annum' interest rate and a payment window of 'fifteen (15) Days'. The changed text only states that late payments 'may accrue interest' and will be paid 'at a time determined by MPL'. According to ORC § 1343.03, the creditor is entitled to interest determined pursuant to section 5703.47 of the Revised Code, unless a written contract provides a different rate of interest. By removing the specific rate, and payment date, the modified contract contradicts the original specific rate and payment date in the original contract.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that the disclosing Party would not have an adequate remedy at law for the breach by the receiving Party of any one or more of the covenants contained in this Section 7 and agrees that, in the event of such breach, the disclosing Party may, in addition to the other remedies that may be available to it, apply to a court for an injunction to prevent breaches of this Section 7 and to enforce specifically the terms and provisions of this Section 7.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that the disclosing Party may have a remedy at law for the breach by the receiving Party of any one or more of the covenants contained in this Section 7 and agrees that, in the event of such breach, the disclosing Party may apply to a court.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the line 'in addition to the other remedies that may be available to it, apply to a court for an injunction to prevent breaches of this Section 7 and to enforce specifically the terms and provisions of this Section 7', the consumer loses the right to seek injunctive relief for breaches of confidentiality, weakening their legal position. This introduces legal risk due to limitations on remedies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ohio Trade Secrets Act, Ohio Revised Code § 1333.63
perturbation.law_explanation: ORC § 1333.63(A) explicitly grants the right to injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. By implying injunctive relief might not be available, the modified contract contradicts this statutory right.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1333.63 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1333.63 | Damages recoverable. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices Effective: July 20, 1994 Latest Legislation: House Bill 320 - 120th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant in a civil action is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages may include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty that is equitable under the circumstances considering the loss to the complainant, the benefit to the misappropriator, or both, for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (B) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award punitive or exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding three times any award made under division (A) of this section. Available Versions of this Section July 20, 1994 – House Bill 320 - 120th General Assembly [ View July 20, 1994 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the explicit right of the disclosing party to seek injunctive relief for breaches of confidentiality, which weakens their legal position. The Law Explanation states that ORC § 1333.63(A) explicitly grants the right to injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets, and the altered contract implies injunctive relief might not be available, contradicting this statutory right. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 1333.63 of the Ohio Revised Code, pertains to damages recoverable for misappropriation, supporting the Law Explanation's claim about trade secret protection, which becomes less enforceable due to the omission of injunctive relief in the modified contract. Therefore, the omission creates a legal contradiction by reducing the remedies available to the disclosing party.

perturbation.original_text: Shipper shall provide to MPL, at any time: (i) upon MPL's request, information ("Financial Information") that will allow MPL to assess (or reassess) and establish creditworthiness and Shipper's capacity to perform any financial obligations that could arise from the transportation of Shipper's Crude Petroleum on the Pipeline; and (ii) upon MPL's good faith determination (which shall be no less than industry standards) of non-creditworthiness, MPL may request, financial assurance in respect of transportation or other services ("Financial Assurances"). Financial Assurances shall be limited to a guarantee from the parent company of Shipper in a form and substance acceptable to MPL and sufficient in amount to cover 6-months of Shipper's obligations to MPL so long as the guarantor has sufficient creditworthiness as set forth in this Section; and if not, MPL may then request an irrevocable standby letter of credit in a form and from an issuer acceptable to MPL, and in an amount no greater than 6-months of tolls based on the Quarterly Volume Commitment, plus all applicable taxes.
perturbation.changed_text: Shipper shall provide to MPL, at any time: (i) upon MPL's request, information that will allow MPL to assess creditworthiness and Shipper's capacity to perform financial obligations; and (ii) MPL may request financial assurance. Financial Assurances will be determined at MPL's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the limitation that the financial assurances shall be no less than industry standards and can be for no greater than 6-months of tolls removes consumer protections and allows the service provider to demand unfair or untenable requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ohio Uniform Commercial Code - Secured Transactions § 1309.203
perturbation.law_explanation: Although pertaining to secured transactions, the general principles of good faith and commercial reasonableness implied in Ohio's UCC (and particularly relevant given the reference to financial assurances) are undermined when the contract removes limitations on what MPL can demand as financial assurance. The deletion opens the door for demands that are commercially unreasonable and not made in good faith, thereby contradicting the spirit and underlying principles of commercial law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1309.203 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1309.203 | Attachment and enforceability of security interest - proceeds - supporting obligation - formal requisites - UCC 9-203. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1309 Secured Transactions Effective: June 29, 2011 Latest Legislation: House Bill 9 - 129th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) A security interest attaches to collateral when it becomes enforceable against the debtor with respect to the collateral, unless an agreement expressly postpones the time of attachment. (B) Except as otherwise provided in divisions (C) to (I) of this section, a security interest is enforceable against the debtor and third parties with respect to the collateral only if: (1) Value has been given; (2) The debtor has rights in the collateral or the power to transfer rights in the collateral to a secured party; and (3) One of the following conditions is met: (a) The debtor has authenticated a security agreement that provides a description of the collateral and, if the security interest covers timber to be cut, a description of the land concerned; (b) The collateral is not a certificated security and is in the possession of the secured party under section 1309.313 of the Revised Code pursuant to the debtor's security agreement; (c) The collateral is a certificated security in registered form and the security certificate has been delivered to the secured party under section 1308.27 of the Revised Code pursuant to the debtor's security agreement; or (d) The collateral is deposit accounts, electronic chattel paper, investment property, letter-of-credit rights, or electronic documents, and the secured party has control under sect
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the industry standard benchmark and the six-month limit on financial assurances, potentially allowing MPL to demand commercially unreasonable or untenable requirements. The original text had consumer protections that the changed text does not. This aligns with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction' because a legally mandated consumer protection has been removed. The Law Explanation supports this by stating, 'the contract removes limitations on what MPL can demand as financial assurance. The deletion opens the door for demands that are commercially unreasonable and not made in good faith, thereby contradicting the spirit and underlying principles of commercial law.'
